commit.message:
Forward Linkup provider options

Let Linkup search and fetch use supported provider-specific
options from config and per-call overrides.

Keep managed fields fixed for pi by rejecting overrides for the
query, maxResults, URL, and non-searchResults output modes.

Also parse Linkup option defaults from config, document the
supported knobs, and use the SDK request types in the adapter.

commit.message:
Simplify startup config errors

Show a concise web-providers config error during startup
instead of letting pi print a stack trace.

When config loading fails on startup, the extension now reports a
single user-facing message and disables managed web tools for that
run so the session can continue.

commit.message:
Scope provider timeout settings

Stop exposing research-only timeout settings on providers that do
not support research. Cloudflare now shows only its provider-
specific fields, and shared request settings are wired through a
capability-aware helper so non-research providers cannot surface a
research timeout by mistake.

commit.message:
Stop preflighting local CLI auth

Stop probing Claude and Codex login state before exposing their
managed tools. The extension now keeps setup checks to obvious
structural errors and lets the underlying CLIs report real auth
failures at runtime.

This removes false negatives from local auth heuristics and keeps
provider availability logic simpler and more consistent.

Closes #12

commit.message:
Use TypeBox for footer config

Validate fancy-footer config strictly with TypeBox instead of
relying on manual coercion. Remove config migration aliases and
fall back to defaults when config validation fails.

Use the same approach for contributed widget metadata while
keeping only the minimal runtime checks for function-valued
fields.

commit.message:
Show only deltas in widget summaries

Widget summaries in the settings screen now omit properties
that match the widget's built-in defaults, showing only what
the user actually customized. Labels are also more readable:
row:, pos:, align:, icon:hidden, and width: replace the old
r/p/w: shorthand.

the pi-coding-agent runtime dependency. Add seven tests.

commit.message:
Rename turn mode to prompt with correct flush semantics

The turn mode flushed after each internal LLM iteration rather
than when the agent finished and yielded back to the user. Rename
it to prompt and wire it to agent_end so formatting runs exactly
once per user-visible response.

Existing formatter.json files that set "formatMode": "turn" are
silently remapped to "prompt" for backwards compatibility.

Also simplify the package.json description to be mode-agnostic.

Add tool, turn, and session modes

Replace the old formatter scheduling options with the new
`tool`, `turn`, and `session` modes and make `turn`
the default.

turn/session batching as sets of unique absolute paths, and
shows formatter summaries uniformly as runner plus cwd-relative
path.

configuration surface.

commit.message:
Port replace to the new executor (#5982)

## 🔍 Problem

- The `replace` operator only had a legacy executor implementation.
- Pipelines running with `--neo` lacked support for `replace`.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Port `replace` to `Operator<table_slice, table_slice>`.
- Register the new path through `Describer`.
- Move the existing integration tests from `test-legacy/` to `test/`.
- Add a changelog entry for `--neo` support.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on parity between the legacy and new executor paths.
- The main risk is preserving split-output behavior and `with=null`
handling.



<sub>Resolves TNZ-167</sub>

commit.message:
Update nvim-treesitter for Neovim 0.12

The nvim-treesitter plugin switched its default branch from master
to main with a completely new API for Neovim 0.12. This adapts the
config accordingly:

- Pin nvim-treesitter to the main branch
- Replace removed configs/parsers API with new setup pattern
- Use vim.treesitter.start() for highlighting via FileType autocmd
- Register TQL parser via User TSUpdate autocmd
- Drop nvim-treesitter-refactor (incompatible with new API)
- Drop legacy TS highlight groups and incremental selection

commit.message:
Fix allocator unused-variable warnings (#6013)

## 🔍 Problem
- Release builds emit `-Wunused-variable` warnings in `allocator.hpp`.
- The variables are only used by allocator assertions, which compile out
when assertions are disabled.

## 🛠️ Solution
- Mark the assertion-only `success` locals as `[[maybe_unused]]`.
- Preserve the existing assertion checks in assertion-enabled builds.

## 💬 Review
- Focus on whether `[[maybe_unused]]` is the right fix for this warning
pattern.
- No changelog entry: this is internal warning cleanup with no
user-facing impact.

commit.message:
Fix Arc with incomplete inner types (#6005)

## 🔍 Problem

- `cmake --build --preset macos-xcode-release` failed in `Subprocess`
  because `Arc` evaluated converting-constructor constraints for `Arc`
  inputs.
- That instantiated `std::constructible_from<T, U>` for incomplete inner
types such as `Subprocess::SharedState` and caused hard compile errors.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Reorder the converting-constructor constraints in `Arc` to reject
`Arc`
  inputs before checking `std::constructible_from<T, U>`.
- This keeps copy and move construction on the dedicated `Arc` overloads
  and avoids constraint instantiation on incomplete types.
- Verified with `cmake --build --preset macos-xcode-release`.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on whether the constraint ordering is the right minimal fix for
  incomplete inner types.
- No user-facing behavior changes; I did not add a changelog entry.

commit.message:
Update vulnerable Python dependencies (#6030)

## 🔍 Problem
- GitHub Dependabot reports open Python alerts for `dynaconf`, `pytest`,
and transitive `Pygments` in `python/uv.lock`.
- The workspace also declared `ruff` and `mypy` settings without making
those tools available through the dev dependency group.

## 🛠️ Solution
- Upgrade `dynaconf` to 3.2.13.
- Move the pytest toolchain to patched releases on Python 3.10+ and
refresh the lockfile.
- Add `ruff` and `mypy` to the Python workspace dev dependencies.
- Configure default mypy targets so `uv run mypy` checks the workspace
packages.

## 💬 Review
- Pay special attention to the Python version markers on the pytest
dependencies.
- `uv run pytest` passes locally.
- `uv run ruff check` and `uv run mypy` now execute, but they still
report pre-existing issues outside this change.

commit.message:
Port more format operators to new executor (#6006)

## 🔍 Problem

- `read_cef`, `read_leef`, `read_zeek_tsv`, and `write_zeek_tsv` still
used the legacy executor path.
- Their operator coverage also still lived partly in `test-legacy/`.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Port `read_cef`, `read_leef`, `read_zeek_tsv`, and `write_zeek_tsv` to
`OperatorPlugin`.
- Keep the legacy plugin entry points for compatibility.
- Migrate the relevant legacy read-operator coverage into `test/`.
- Reuse shared fixtures from `test/inputs/` where possible.
- Update touched file headers to the current Tenzir banner.

## 💬 Review

- Review the new executor implementations in `cef.cpp`, `leef.cpp`, and
`zeek_tsv.cpp`.
- The migrated tests should make it easier to compare behavior with the
old path.

commit.message:
Refresh grafted precompiled headers

Copied PCH files still point at the source worktree, so later
single-file rebuilds in the grafted worktree can fail with
duplicate definitions. Rebuild the copied PCH targets locally
after fixing the build tree.

Restore the original mtimes after rebuilding so dependent object
files keep their cached timestamps and grafted builds stay fast.

commit.message:
Fix graft hashes for custom Ninja commands

ninja -t compdb only covers compile edges, so graft still missed
custom COMMAND entries stored in .ninja_log.

Parse build.ninja to recover those commands too and record both
relative and absolute output paths. This lets graft preserve hashes
for generated files, symlink updates, and other custom steps so a
fresh Tenzir worktree matches the source build's first-build
behavior.

commit.message:
Fix grafting for nested Ninja builds

Handle nested preset directories such as build/xcode/release as
real Ninja build roots when repairing grafted state.

Also make path rewriting idempotent so rerunning graft does not
double-rewrite target paths and invalidate Ninja command hashes.
This avoids the full rebuild triggered after switching Tenzir
worktrees with worktrunk.

commit.message:
Port `yara` to the new executor and remove `blockwise` (#6035)

## 🔍 Problem

- `yara` was still only wired up for the old executor.
- The port carried forward a `blockwise` mode that exposed chunk
boundaries instead of treating the input as one logical byte stream.
- The TQL2 form only accepted rule paths as a list literal.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Port `yara` to the new executor.
- Remove the `blockwise` flag from both executor paths.
- Keep one execution model: buffer the full byte stream in memory and
scan once at end of input.
- Accept both `yara "rule.yar"` and `yara ["rule-a.yar", "rule-b.yar"]`.
- Drop the obsolete `blockwise` integration test and add coverage for
both rule argument spellings.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on the new executor implementation in
`plugins/yara/src/plugin.cpp`.
- The intended semantics are now strictly whole-stream and finite-input
only.
- The new argument helper is shared between validation and operator
construction.

## Documentation PR

- https://github.com/tenzir/docs/pull/261

Port `yara` to the new executor and remove `blockwise` (#6035)

## 🔍 Problem

- `yara` was still only wired up for the old executor.
- The port carried forward a `blockwise` mode that exposed chunk
boundaries instead of treating the input as one logical byte stream.
- The TQL2 form only accepted rule paths as a list literal.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Port `yara` to the new executor.
- Remove the `blockwise` flag from both executor paths.
- Keep one execution model: buffer the full byte stream in memory and
scan once at end of input.
- Accept both `yara "rule.yar"` and `yara ["rule-a.yar", "rule-b.yar"]`.
- Drop the obsolete `blockwise` integration test and add coverage for
both rule argument spellings.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on the new executor implementation in
`plugins/yara/src/plugin.cpp`.
- The intended semantics are now strictly whole-stream and finite-input
only.
- The new argument helper is shared between validation and operator
construction.

## Documentation PR

- https://github.com/tenzir/docs/pull/261

<sub>resolves TNZ-150</sub>

commit.message:
Fix no-op build stamp handling (#6029)

## 🔍 Problem

- Incremental builds still did unnecessary work because several
generated artifacts were modeled as side-effecting custom targets
instead of build outputs.
- Tightening those rules exposed a few places where the invalidation
semantics are intentionally different:
  - schema deletions could leave stale copied files in the build tree
- `compile_commands.json` is one shared source-tree symlink, so multiple
build dirs can race on it
  - `config.cpp` must keep tracking `git describe --dirty`
- bundled Proxygen declares `StatsWrapper.h` in the build tree, but its
generator writes it to the source tree

## 🛠️ Solution

- Keep stable generated artifacts on output-based rules where that
matches the actual build semantics.
- Make bundled schema copying invalidate on deletions via a manifest.
- Keep `libtenzir` config generation as an always-run target so
Git-derived version metadata stays current.
- Keep compilation database relinking as a custom target so each build
dir can refresh the shared source-tree symlink.
- Materialize Proxygen's `StatsWrapper.h` in Tenzir's build tree via a
dependency-tracked target.
- Disable bundled `clickhouse-cpp` debug helper targets.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on the places where this PR intentionally does **not** use plain
output-based rules: `libtenzir_update_config`, `compilation-database`,
and the Proxygen `StatsWrapper.h` workaround.
- No changelog entry: this is an internal build-system-only change.
- Verified locally with repeated configure/build cycles and after
CI-driven fixes for clean-checkout and subproject cases.

commit.message:
Add hostname resolution to accept_tcp (#6017)

## 🔍 Problem

- `accept_tcp` no longer matched `load_tcp` for peer hostname
enrichment.
- `$peer.ip` in the new TCP operators was exposed as a string instead of
an
  `ip` value.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Add an async reverse-DNS helper in `libtenzir/async/`.
- Add `resolve_hostnames=true` to `accept_tcp` and expose
`$peer.hostname`.
- Make `$peer.ip` use the native `ip` type in both `accept_tcp` and
  `from_tcp`.
- Add integration coverage.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on the async lookup boundary and message flow in `accept_tcp`.
- The helper currently uses `spawn_blocking(...)`, so it avoids blocking
the
EventBase and operator state machine while staying reusable for a future
  `from_udp` port.

## Documentation PR

- https://github.com/tenzir/docs/pull/258

<sub>resolves TNZ-498</sub>

output.

regression.

commit.message:
Harden installer CI failure notification (#6049)

## 🔍 Problem

- The installer workflow paged Discord for an Ubuntu mirror/network
flake in container bootstrap.
- `notify-failure` treated any matrix failure as an installer
regression, even when the installer never ran.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Split installer checks into explicit download and execution steps.
- Add retries for bootstrap package installs and installer downloads.
- Make Debian bootstrap fail on partial `apt-get update` results instead
of continuing with stale indices.
- Inspect failed job steps before notifying Discord, and only alert when
installer download or execution failed.

## 💬 Review

- The main behavior change is in the notification classifier: setup-only
failures now keep the workflow red but skip Discord.
- I did not add a changelog entry because this is an internal CI-only
change.

commit.message:
Make summarize frequency emission deterministic (#6042)

## 🔍 Problem

- `summarize` with `options={frequency: ...}` can emit periodic results
nondeterministically.
- The neo implementation lets the timer path race with `process()`, so
rows that arrive after a deadline can be counted in the earlier
emission.
- The schedule is also anchored at operator startup instead of the first
input event.

restored state already contains input.

cannot slip into earlier results.

instead of mutating operator state from `await_task()`.
- Add a regression test for first-input anchoring and include a bugfix
changelog entry.

- Focus on the timer coordination in `Summarize` and the catch-up logic
in the legacy path.

covers the startup-anchoring regression.

commit.message:
Keep bundled wangle headers ahead of Homebrew (#6069)

## 🔍 Problem

- Building with bundled Facebook libraries can still pick up Homebrew
`wangle` headers.
- That lets proxygen compile against a different `ManagedConnection` API
than the bundled `wangle` archive provides.
- On arm64 macOS this shows up as an undefined
`wangle::ManagedConnection::onConnectionAgeTimeout()` at link time.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Stop adding the vendored `wangle` subdirectory as a `SYSTEM`
dependency.
- Keep the bundled `wangle` headers ahead of Homebrew in the compiler
search order.
- Leave the rest of the bundled Facebook stack unchanged.

## 💬 Review

- Main thing to check: using a narrow `wangle`-only fix instead of
broader include-order rewrites.
- Validated with:
- `cmake --build build/xcode/release --target proxygen_coro --config
Release`
  - `cmake --build build/xcode/release --target tenzir --config Release`

Move sources into src and fix reload crash

Move the extension sources and package metadata from the legacy
extensions/ layout into src/ so the published package exports,
tests, and extension entry point all point at one source tree.

after session replacement or reload. The footer now guards old
async refresh callbacks, resolves the thinking level from session
entries, and uses loader-compatible type syntax so pi can parse
the extension again.

Add a changelog entry for the footer crash fix.

commit.message:
Fix unroll crash on large expansions (#6075)

## 🔍 Problem

- `unroll` could accumulate a large expanded list into one Arrow array.
- Once duplicated surrounding data exceeded Arrow's per-array capacity,
the operator hit an internal assertion instead of producing output.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Emit bounded output slices while expanding list values.
- Keep the existing schema, ordering, and null/empty-list behavior
intact.
- Add a regression that expands a list past the default table-slice size
and checks that output is split while preserving row count.

## 💬 Review

- The main trade-off is the conservative byte estimate used for
flushing; row count remains capped by the existing table-slice size.
- Arrow builder failures in this path now surface as diagnostics instead
of assertions.

<sub>
✅ Closes TNZ-334
</sub>

commit.message:
Skip unreadable directories in recursive files traversal (#6073) ## 🔍 Problem - `files , recurse=true` failed when traversal encountered an unreadable child directory. - The operator was still missing from the neo executor path. - Follow-up review found that the neo source buffered the full traversal and that failed status lookups could produce bogus permission metadata. ## 🛠️ Solution - Skip unreadable child directories during recursive traversal, emit a warning by default, and continue with accessible siblings. - Treat `skip_permission_denied=true` as ignoring permission-denied paths silently: unreadable initial directories produce no events, and recursive child-directory warnings are suppressed. - Add a neo `files` source implementation that reuses the same traversal and event-building helpers, offloads filesystem work to the blocking executor, and emits slices incrementally. - Leave `type` and `permissions` null when filesystem status lookup fails, instead of deriving permissions from an unknown status. ## 💬 Review - The `files` test now runs with the default neo runner. - The legacy path still exists for compatibility and shares the same traversal helpers. - The fixture covers both unreadable child directories and dangling symlinks. 📚 Docs PR: tenzir/docs#277
✅ Closes TNZ-516, TNZ-520

commit.message:
Only save ccache after compilation starts (#6072)

## 🔍 Problem

- `Save ccache Cache` currently uses `if: always()`, so macOS CI saves a
new SHA-keyed cache even when the job is cancelled or fails before
compilation starts.
- On PRs, `cancel-in-progress` makes that case common: a run can be
cancelled during dependency setup or configure, never produce new object
files, and still publish a cache entry.
- That entry is not warmer than the previously restored cache, but it
can still become the newest branch-prefix candidate and consumes part of
the 10 GB GitHub Actions cache budget.

**Key benefit:** keep the useful partial caches from failed or cancelled
compiles, while stopping pre-compile cancellations and setup/configure
failures from publishing low-value ccache entries.

| Scenario | Today (`if: always()`) | This PR | Why this is better |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Job is cancelled during setup/dependency install | Saves cache anyway
| Does not save | No compile happened, so the cache is not warmer than
before. |
| Configure step fails before compile | Saves cache anyway | Does not
save | Avoids publishing a new SHA cache with no new object files. |
| Compile starts, then fails halfway | Saves cache | Saves cache | Keeps
the useful partial-build case. |
| Compile starts, then job is cancelled mid-build | Saves cache | Saves
cache | Preserves warmed objects from the partial compile. |
| Compile succeeds | Saves cache | Saves cache | No regression. |
| Exact cache hit for same SHA | Tries to save again | Skips save |
Avoids a pointless save step. |

## 🛠️ Solution

- Give the restore and compile steps IDs so later steps can distinguish
"compile ran" from "compile never started".
- Gate `Show ccache Statistics` and `Save ccache Cache` on
`steps.compile_all_targets.outcome != 'skipped'`.
- Keep `always()` so successful, failed, and mid-compile cancelled
builds still save their partial ccache state.
- Reuse `steps.ccache_restore.outputs.cache-primary-key` and skip saving
on an exact cache hit.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on whether `outcome != 'skipped'` matches the intended policy
boundary: save after any compile attempt, but not after pre-compile
cancellation or setup/configure failure.
- This PR intentionally does not change the cache key shape or workflow
concurrency. It only improves which runs are allowed to publish a cache.
- No changelog entry: this is an internal CI behavior fix without
user-facing changes.

<sub>
📎 Related: tenzir/tenzir#6065
</sub>

| Job is cancelled during setup/dependency install | Saves cache anyway
| Does not save | No compile happened, so the cache is not warmer than
before. |
| Configure step fails before compile | Saves cache anyway | Does not
save | Avoids publishing a new SHA cache with no new object files. |
| Compile starts, then fails halfway | Saves cache | Saves cache | Keeps
the useful partial-build case. |
| Compile starts, then job is cancelled mid-build | Saves cache | Saves
cache | Preserves warmed objects from the partial compile. |
| Compile succeeds | Saves cache | Saves cache | No regression. |
| Exact cache hit for same SHA | Tries to save again | Skips save |
Avoids a pointless save step. |

## 🛠️ Solution

- Give the restore and compile steps IDs so later steps can distinguish
"compile ran" from "compile never started".
- Gate `Show ccache Statistics` and `Save ccache Cache` on
`steps.compile_all_targets.outcome != 'skipped'`.
- Keep `always()` so successful, failed, and mid-compile cancelled
builds still save their partial ccache state.
- Reuse `steps.ccache_restore.outputs.cache-primary-key` and skip saving
on an exact cache hit.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on whether `outcome != 'skipped'` matches the intended policy
boundary: save after any compile attempt, but not after pre-compile
cancellation or setup/configure failure.
- This PR intentionally does not change the cache key shape or workflow
concurrency. It only improves which runs are allowed to publish a cache.
- No changelog entry: this is an internal CI behavior fix without
user-facing changes.

<sub>
📎 Related: tenzir/tenzir#6065
</sub>

commit.message:
Port from_ftp and to_ftp to the new executor (#6044) ## 🔍 Problem - `from_ftp` and `to_ftp` still relied on the legacy executor path. - FTP transfers needed an executor-native curl path with backpressure and cancellation instead of blocking worker threads. ## 🛠️ Solution - Add new-executor `from_ftp` and `to_ftp` operators with required parser/printer subpipelines. - Introduce an async curl adapter around prepared `curl::easy` handles, plus dedicated upload/download helpers. - Propagate cancellation into the curl transfer and body adapters, and add FTP regression coverage for early exit, upload aborts, and local printer failure. ## 💬 Review - Please focus on async curl teardown and cancellation semantics, plus the operator lifecycle around subpipeline shutdown. - This ports the direct `from_ftp` and `to_ftp` operators only; generic `from "ftp://..."` and `to "ftp://..."` still go through the legacy connector path. - No changelog entry in this PR, per request. 📚 Docs PR: tenzir/docs#265
✅ Resolves TNZ-132, TNZ-133

commit.message:
Port load_balance to the new executor (#6077)

## 🔍 Problem

- `load_balance` still only had a legacy executor implementation.
- The neo compiler also needed to preserve the operator-specific `$`
list expansion semantics.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Add a custom IR path that expands the worker list into sink
subpipelines.
- Implement whole-slice dispatch to the least-loaded active worker, with
runtime-only scheduling counters.
- Add neo integration coverage and turn invalid source subpipelines into
type diagnostics instead of assertions.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on subpipeline shutdown/finalization and the decision to avoid
snapshotting scheduler hints.
- Distribution is intentionally whole-slice least-loaded, not the legacy
demand-based handoff.

<sub>
✅ Closes TNZ-46
</sub>

Add NATS JetStream operators (#6047) ## 🔍 Problem - Tenzir does not have native operators for consuming from or publishing to NATS JetStream. - Users need a way to consume JetStream messages while preserving NATS metadata alongside the emitted payload. ## 🛠️ Solution - Add `from_nats` and `to_nats` operators built on `nats.c` JetStream APIs. - Support NATS URL configuration, authentication records, the standard Tenzir `tls` record, message headers, and a `metadata_field` record with NATS/JetStream metadata. - Add Docker-backed NATS fixtures and integration tests for source, sink, validation, and round-trip behavior. - Wire `cnats` into CMake and development dependency scripts with feature checks for the required JetStream APIs. ## 💬 Review - Focus on the callback-to-coroutine handoff in `from_nats`, especially queue capacity, stop handling, and message ack/nak behavior. - Check the `to_nats` publish acknowledgement handling and error reporting. - Verified locally with: - `scripts/build.sh` - `uvx tenzir-test --root test --match 'operators/from_nats' --match 'operators/to_nats' --debug` - `uvx ruff check test/tests/operators/from_nats/count_backpressure/consume.py` 📚 Docs PR: tenzir/docs#266

commit.message:
Add Splunk HEC metadata and raw endpoint support (#6074) ## 🔍 Problem - The neo `to_splunk` path could only send HEC event envelopes. - Users could not set HEC `_time`, attach indexed HEC fields, or send already-formatted log text to Splunk's raw HEC endpoint. - Raw HEC metadata is request-level, so batching has to respect metadata changes. - The legacy `to_splunk` implementation should remain untouched. ## 🛠️ Solution - Add neo `to_splunk` support for `time=...` and event-mode `fields={...}`. - Keep HEC event envelopes as the default; `event=...` selects the envelope payload and defaults to `this`. - Add raw HEC mode with the mutually exclusive `raw=` option instead of an endpoint selector. - Send raw events to `/services/collector/raw` with request-level metadata query parameters and a generated request channel. - Split adjacent raw batches when request metadata changes, preserve raw pending events across compression failures, and keep raw metadata aligned when null raw rows are skipped. - Add validation tests, live Splunk coverage, and a changelog entry. ## 💬 Review - Check the final `event=` / `raw=` UX and validation behavior. - Check raw batching around metadata changes and null raw rows. - Check `time` and `fields` validation, warning, and omission behavior. - The `contrib/tenzir-plugins` submodule points at the companion plugin commit on `tenzir/tenzir-plugins:topic/to-splunk-improvements`. 🧩 Plugin PR: tenzir/tenzir-plugins#520
📚 Docs PR: tenzir/docs#278

commit.message:
Fix Python operator error hangs (#6095)

## 🔍 Problem

- Python operator tests can hang when the Python subprocess exits with
an error.
- The host waits for Arrow output while the child writes diagnostics to
its error pipe.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Close the Python executor stdout stream before writing error details.
- This lets the host observe EOF and then drain the dedicated error
pipe.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on subprocess lifecycle for syntax errors and runtime
exceptions.
- The existing Python operator integration tests cover the regression.

commit.message:
Speed up ternary expression matrix test (#6091)

## 🔍 Problem

- `tests/operators/if/ternary_expr/all_paths.sh` timed out in the shell
runner.
- The traced pipelines finish correctly, but the test intentionally
starts Tenzir for every matrix entry to compare default and eager
evaluation.
- On slower machines, that matrix can exceed the shell runner's default
30s wall-clock budget.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Use the shell-runner frontmatter to raise this test's timeout to 120s.
- This keeps the Tenzir invocations unchanged, preserving the existing
coverage while giving the matrix enough time to complete.

## 💬 Review

- Please check whether 120s is an appropriate budget for this matrix
test.
- No changelog or docs update: this is test-only and does not change
user-facing behavior.

commit.message:
Stabilize accept_http readiness tests (#6092)

## 🔍 Problem

- `accept_http` integration tests can send fixture requests before the
listener is ready.
- The deterministic fixture port allocator can also pick ports with IPv6
wildcard listeners, which only show up as connectable after allocation.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Wait for the fixture endpoint to accept TCP connections before sending
HTTP requests.
- Preserve `initial_delay` as the earliest time the first request may be
sent.
- Reject TCP ports that are already connectable when allocating fixture
ports.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on the fixture synchronization semantics: no explicit readiness
timeout, teardown stops the worker.

optional Azure Blob Storage plugin is unavailable because the Python

missing.

commit.message:
Unify agent build instructions (#6083)

## 🔍 Problem

- PR #5921 restored a Claude-specific session hook so Claude could find
local build binaries.
- `tenzir-test` now has project hooks, so binary selection can live with
the test project instead of agent-specific configuration.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Add a `test/hooks` startup hook that selects `tenzir` and
`tenzir-node` from the local build before test discovery.
- Keep `scripts/build.sh` focused on building targets, with a minimal
`--print-build-dir` mode for the hook.
- Update `AGENTS.md` to describe the intended workflow: build first,
then run `uvx tenzir-test --root test`.
- Remove the Claude-specific command, session hook, and permissions
config.

## 💬 Review

- Please focus on whether the hook behavior is the right place to select
local test binaries and whether the agent instructions are sufficiently
workflow-oriented.

commit.message:
Reject invalid TLS probe prefixes

Keep partial probes on the TLS path only while the bytes remain compatible with a ClientHello. Once a byte contradicts the TLS header shape, the connection returns to the plaintext path instead of waiting for the rest of the probe or forcing a TLS upgrade after a timeout. Add a split-payload regression fixture for non-TLS data that starts with the TLS record byte.

commit.message:
Require Arrow 24 for S3 filesystem stability (#6100)

## 🔍 Problem

- Constructing Arrow/AWS S3 filesystems can segfault with Arrow 23.0.1
and the current AWS SDK stack.
- The crash affects S3-backed operators such as `from_s3`, `to_s3`, and
`to_amazon_security_lake` before useful S3 diagnostics can be emitted.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Require Arrow 24.0 or newer.
- Build Arrow 24.0.0 in both Nix and Debian/Docker dependency packaging
paths.
- Keep the S3 failure mode as a normal filesystem/network diagnostic
instead of a process crash.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on whether all build paths that pin Arrow are covered.
- I found the Docker image path through
`scripts/debian/build-arrow-package.sh` and updated it alongside the Nix
override.
- Homebrew already provides Arrow 24.0.0.

commit.message:
Install TLS test dependencies (#6089)

## 🔍 Problem

- Platform TLS/proxy integration suites rely on `trustme` for
certificate generation.
- Container-based `tenzir-test` invocations did not install that fixture
dependency, so the suites were skipped as unavailable.
- Once enabled, the platform tests could race on the default node
endpoint port during parallel integration runs.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Install `trustme` inline for the affected `uvx`/`uv tool run`
`tenzir-test` invocations.
- Disable the node-to-node listener in the platform connection tests,
since they only exercise the platform client.

## 💬 Review

- Please check whether inline `uv --with trustme` is the preferred scope
for this fixture-only dependency.

commit.message:
Ack NATS messages before timeout (#6096)

## 🔍 Problem

- `from_nats` could hold a delivered JetStream message until the batch
timeout.
- With short ACK waits, the message could be redelivered before Tenzir
sent the ACK.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Wake the source task with an explicit ACK control message.
- Flush pending ACKs immediately on the ACK handoff path.
- Bound the wait for additional batch members after a message is already
held.

## 💬 Review

- Focus on the ACK handoff path and the interaction with early
downstream stop.
- No changelog per request.

commit.message:
Retry Kafka offset commits (#6090)

## 🔍 Problem

- `from_kafka` could surface transient offset commit warnings while the
Kafka group coordinator was still settling.
- In fixture runs this showed up as a flaky `Broker: Not coordinator`
warning after successfully consuming the expected message.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Retry transient coordinator-related offset commit errors before
emitting a warning.
- Keep offsets pending when a commit still fails so a later checkpoint
can retry them instead of dropping local commit state.

## 💬 Review

- The retry set is limited to coordinator/readiness errors from
librdkafka.
- Non-transient commit errors still warn immediately.

commit.message:
Port serve tests to tenzir-test (#6099)

## 🔍 Problem

- The `serve` operator coverage still lived in `test-legacy`.
- The legacy tests depended on the deprecated multi runner and custom
Python orchestration.

## 🛠️ Solution

- Port the coverage to native `tenzir-test` tests under
`test/tests/operators/serve`.
- Configure serve pipelines through the suite-local node config and
consume them with TQL `api` tests.
- Cover both `/serve` and `/serve-multi` without Python helpers or
hardcoded CLI switches.

## 💬 Review

- Check that the suite-level retry and node-configured serve pipelines
capture the intended legacy coverage.
- The new TQL fetch tests request all events at once instead of walking
continuation tokens.
